title: NOC 31103 – Veterinarians | GTR Immigration
description: Veterinarians play a critical role in animal health, food safety, public health, and agricultural sustainability across Canada. With increasing pet ownership, expanding livestock industries, and strengthened biosecurity regulations, demand for qualified veterinarians continues to rise nationwide. Classified under NOC 31103 (TEER 1), this occupation aligns with:
duties: Diagnose diseases in individual animals, herds, and flocks
Conduct laboratory tests and physical examinations
Vaccinate animals and implement disease-prevention programs
Advise clients on animal nutrition, housing, breeding, and hygiene
Provide obstetrics, dentistry, and euthanasia services
Supervise veterinary technologists and support staff
Enforce government disease-control and food-production regulations
Conduct veterinary research in nutrition, pharmaceuticals, and disease control
employment_requirements: 4–5 year university degree in veterinary medicine
Successful completion of national certification exams
Additional 3–4 years postgraduate training or residency
Accreditation by the American College of Veterinary Internal Medicine or American Board of Veterinary Practitioners
In Quebec, accreditation by the Ordre des médecins vétérinaires du Québec
job_outlook: This suggests strong demand, particularly in:

pathways: Opportunities in food safety and public health
Competitive salaries with ownership potential in private clinics

additional_information: Private animal hospitals and clinicsLivestock farms and agricultural operationsGovernment inspection agenciesFood safety and regulatory authoritiesResearch laboratories and pharmaceutical companiesZoos and wildlife organizations
Typical annual earnings: $30,300–$139,700 CAD, depending on province, specialization, and practice type.
Rural livestock veterinarians and small-animal specialists are particularly in demand.
